coredata.dc:title: Morphological diversity effect of graphene quantum dot/MIL88A(Fe) composites on dye and pharmaceuticals (tetracycline and doxycycline) removal
coredata.dc:creator: Rabeie, Bahareh
Mahmoodi, Niyaz Mohammad
Mahkam, Mehrdad
coredata.dc:description: Herein, composites with different morphologies (diamond-like, rod-like, and spindle-like) were successfully synthesized using MIL88A (Fe) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to remove Acid Blue 92 (AB92), pharmaceuticals (doxycycline, and tetracycline). Despite the similarity in content, chemical structure, and functional groups of the composites, interesting differences were observed in some of their physicochemical properties (BET, RAMAN, TEM, and SEM) and adsorption performance, which was the turning point of this research. Spindle-like GQDMIL88A composite with the adsorption capacity of 617 mg/g indicated the highest adsorption capacity for Acid Blue 92 (AB92) compared to 335 mg/g for rod-like composites and 114 mg/g for diamond-like composites. Also, for doxycycline removal, the spindle-like composite with the adsorption capacity of 796 mg/g, was still in the first rank, compared to 588 mg/g for rod-like and 469 mg/g for diamond-like GQDMIL88A. The tetracycline adsorption capacity for spindle-like, rod-like, and diamond-like GQDMIL88A composites was 672 mg/g, 398 mg/g, and 300 mg/g, respectively. According to the findings of this study, despite the different adsorption capacities of different GQDMIL88A composite morphologies, all three types are well compatible with the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetics.

head.ce:title: Morphological diversity effect of graphene quantum dot/MIL88A(Fe) composites on dye and pharmaceuticals (tetracycline and doxycycline) removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Research, Institute for Color Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Research, Institute for Color Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Research, Institute for Color Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, composites with different morphologies (diamond-like, rod-like, and spindle-like) were successfully synthesized using MIL88A (Fe) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to remove Acid Blue 92 (AB92), pharmaceuticals (doxycycline, and tetracycline). Despite the similarity in content, chemical structure, and functional groups of the composites, interesting differences were observed in some of their physicochemical properties (BET, RAMAN, TEM, and SEM) and adsorption performance, which was the turning point of this research. Spindle-like GQDMIL88A composite with the adsorption capacity of 617 mg/g indicated the highest adsorption capacity for Acid Blue 92 (AB92) compared to 335 mg/g for rod-like composites and 114 mg/g for diamond-like composites. Also, for doxycycline removal, the spindle-like composite with the adsorption capacity of 796 mg/g, was still in the first rank, compared to 588 mg/g for rod-like and 469 mg/g for diamond-like GQDMIL88A. The tetracycline adsorption capacity for spindle-like, rod-like, and diamond-like GQDMIL88A composites was 672 mg/g, 398 mg/g, and 300 mg/g, respectively. According to the findings of this study, despite the different adsorption capacities of different GQDMIL88A composite morphologies, all three types are well compatible with the Langmuir isotherm and pseudo-second-order kinetics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The expansion of industry and the development of human societies have raised significant concerns about the high volume of wastewater containing toxic substances, high COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand), and degradation-resistant compounds. Wastewater from various industries, such as pharmaceuticals, textiles, paper, leather, cosmetics, etc. is increasing every year. Peculiarly, the depletion of pharmacological materials, textile dyes, and related substances can have many carcinogenic, mutagenic and allergenic effects and may even disrupt the photosynthetic or biological activity of aquatic living organisms. Since these compounds usually have complex and very stable aromatic structures. Therefore, many researchers are working tirelessly to achieve efficient and low-cost technology to eliminate these pollutants [1–6].

Various methods have been proposed for wastewater treatment. Chemical, physical and biological processes (anaerobic-aerobic biological treatment, fungal culture, membrane bioreactor (MBR), etc.) can be mentioned. Among these methods, the adsorption process is known as a popular prominent method due to its simple design, cost-effectiveness, ease of use, non-secondary pollution, energy efficiency, and minimal sludge formation [7–10].

The efficient and appropriate design for the removal of contaminants requires the identification of characteristics (size, load, performance, structural shape, pH, hydrophobicity, and adsorbent surface) as well as an understanding of the physicochemical structure of adsorbents and contaminants. Knowing the mechanisms of the adsorption process (π-π interactions, acid-base interactions, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, etc.) can also be another determining factor [11,12]. This work offers new insights into the design of adsorbents with the same chemical structure and different morphology.

The binding of metal clusters or ions and tunable organic ligands create excellent materials with a wide lattice and potential porosity called metal-organic frameworks (MOFs). Over the last decade, these hybrid porous materials have attracted the outstanding attention of many researchers, due to their special architecture and physicochemical properties, wide surface area, abundant unsaturated sites, chemical and thermal resistance, accessible cages, changeable openings, and high specific gravity. A wide range of their applications in various fields, including gas storage [13], sensors [14], electrical application [15], catalysis [16], photodegradation [17], adsorption [18], etc. is the reason to confirm the importance of the specific compounds. Recently, various adsorbents based on MOFs have been designed and evaluated for the removal of dye [19–21] and pharmacological contaminants [22–24]. It should be emphasized that the Fe-based non-toxic MOFs synthesized are suitable for optimizing research due to their low cost, favorable physicochemical properties, environmental compatibility, and easy access to organic bonds [25].

One of the structural wonders of MOF, namely the synthesis of one MOF type with diverse morphology resulting from different growth of different MOF crystal surfaces and comparing their performance despite having the same functional groups and chemical composition, has recently become a turning point for the research of some scientists [26,27].

By reducing the width of graphene to 10 nm, and restricting the movement of electrons in it, the effect of quantum confinement occurs, and a new generation of nanoscale graphene, called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) emerged. Quantum dots as 0D nanomaterials would be superior to their counterparts contain a higher surface/volume ratio, significant solubility in aqueous and organic solvents, and can possess better adjustability in physico-chemical properties and pliability to hybridize with other materials, also tremendous potential for doping and functionalization [28]. Depending on their synthesis method, GQDs have a diameter of less than 20 nm and a thickness of about 0.5–1.0 nm [29,30]. GQDs are effective tools in a variety of scientific fields, including energy [25], electronics [31], photonics, luminescence [32,33], medicine [34,35] and adsorption. Although GQD had received a great deal of attention from the scientific community due to its amazing optical properties, the importance of these nanomaterial's effect on the adsorption process has been recently evaluated. Indeed discovering their remarkable potential of them in the adsorption of pollutants [36,37] and improving the properties of other adsorbents such as aerogel [38], halloysite [39], MOF [40], montmorillonite [41], and hydroxyapatite [42] can be a precious achievement.

So far, frequently graphene and MOF composites to improve the pristine material performance in various fields have been evaluated [43]. New composites with a combined GQD and MOF structure have been developed to modify, enhance, or create new properties in the resulting material [31,44].

Acid Blue 92 is used as an anionic azo dye in a wide range of industries including textiles (for dyeing silk, polyamide fibers, and wool) and leather, paper, soap, etc. [45]. Despite its various applications, its chemical stability and tenacious nature have made it a threat to human health and the environment. Therefore, the removal of this contaminant studied in this work can be a beneficial step toward improving the condition of textile effluents.

The tetracycline family, as the low-cost and common antibiotics, have shown extensive antimicrobial properties for veterinary medicine as well as human infections. Also to promote growth, and prevent disease in livestock an additive, is added to their feed. Since it is not able to be fully metabolized in living organisms, approximately 90% of tetracycline used, is released into the environment through living urine. This excretion of tetracycline may increase antimicrobial resistance and pose a threat to the transmission of antimicrobial bacteria [46,47]. Therefore, the removal of this pollutant from the environment would be a necessity. Several methods have been proposed to remove tetracycline. In this regard can be noted biological treatment [48], advanced oxidation [49], and adsorption [50]. Table S1 (Supplementary file) compares the adsorption properties of our synthesized adsorbents with other MIL88A composites to emphasize this research efficiency [50–54].

A literature review indicated that GQD/MIL88A composite was not used to remove dye and pharmaceutical pollutants. Herein, GQD/MIL88A composites with different morphologies (diamond-like, rod-like, and spindle-like morphologies) were synthesized and their structural, characteristic, and functional differences were studied. The synthesized adsorbents were used to remove Acid Blue 92 (AB92), pharmaceuticals (doxycycline, and tetracycline). The effect of operational parameters on pollutant removal was investigated. In addition, adsorption isotherm, kinetics, pollutant removal, and adsorbent regeneration were studied in detail.
The used materials were inserted in the supplementary file. The molecular structure and properties of AB92, Tetracycline, and Doxycycline are summarized in Fig. S1 and Table S2, respectively.
The synthesis method of GQD was inserted in the supplementary file [55,56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of three morphologies of MIL88A (Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis methods of three morphologies of MIL88A (Fe) were inserted in the supplementary file [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of different content of GQD in the rod-like GQD MIL88A composite
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, a certain amount of GQD was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water for 15 min by sonication (Fig. 2). In another container, FeCl3.6 H2O (5 mmol, 1.352 g) and fumaric acid (5 mmol, 0.580 g) were added to 25 mL of DI water and stirred constantly with a magnetic stirrer. After obtaining a homogeneous and uniform mixture, the GQD solution was gradually added to the solution and stirred for 1 h. The resulting solution was autoclaved at 65 °C for 12 h. The final products were the r-GQDMIL88A (1%), r-GQDMIL88 (5%), r-GQDMIL88A (9%), and r-GQDMIL88A (11%) (The prefix (r) denotes rod-like GQD MIL88A and the end numbers indicate the weight percent of GQD added, according to the pristine MIL88A yield) [57,58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of diverse morphologies of GQD MIL88A (Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to synthesize different morphologies of GQDMIL88A, the solvent used in the synthesis process was changed (deionized water, N, N-Dimethyl formamide (DMF), and DI water 1: 1 DMF). The result of this effort was the formation of rod-like, diamond-like, and spindle-like GQDMIL-88A morphologies, respectively. All synthesis steps are similar to the production of GQDMIL88A(1%) composite. It should be noted in the rest of the article (The prefix (s) denotes spindle-like GQDMIL88A and (d) denotes diamond-like GQDMIL88A, and (r) denotes rod-like GQD MIL88A). The schematic of the synthesis method was presented in Fig. S2 of the supplementary file.

To investigate the material functional groups, FTIR spectroscopy was used. Powder samples with KBr reference material were grounded to complete softening and then pressed. The tablet thus formed, was exposed to IR light and the spectrum of light transmission (or absorption) in the range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 was examined. The morphology of materials was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) LEO 1455VP. To study the crystalline structure of our synthesized material, the XRD model Siemens D-5000 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 A°) has been applied by measurement similar to the previous reports. Features and defects of materials synthesized by placement 532 laser irradiation in the device (Takram P50C0R10 model Teksan spectrometer) were evaluated. For GQD synthesis, Microwave equipment (SynthWAVE, Italy) was used. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area and materials pore volume measurement were done through N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms by a micrometric surface area and porosity analyzer (ASAP-2020). Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were provided by TEM Philips EM 208 S (Vmax=100 Kv). The residual pollutants concentration in the samples was measured by the double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer). To collect the sediment of the synthesized samples and also to separate the adsorbents containing contaminants from the sampled solutions, we applied a universal 320 centrifuge 1–12000 rpm. Zeta potential analysis was evaluated using HORIBA, SZ-100V2 Series. Also, the METTLER TOLEDO device was used to adjust the pH.

The characterization of materials was inserted in the supplementary file. Adsorption experiments were carried out by adding 7 mg of the adsorbents to flasks containing 100 mL of contaminant solution. The adsorption rate of all adsorbents was measured at specified intervals during the adsorption process. Finally, the adsorbent containing the contaminant was separated from the solution using centrifugation and the concentration of the remaining contaminant in the solution was combined using the UV–VIS CECIL 2021 dual-beam spectrometer at the maximum wavelengths of the adsorbents (presented in Table S1). Compared and analyzed using the calibration curve. Finally, the amount of pollutant uptake (٪) was calculated [59]. To clarify the effect of different parameters in the adsorption process, the initial concentration of Acid Blue 92 solution (20–50 mg/L), different doses of adsorbents (2–9 mg in 100 mL), the pH of the initial solution which was adjusted by dilute HCl and NaOH, different weight ratios of GQD (1–11 wt%) in the synthesized rod-like GQDMIL88A composite, the effect of different morphologies of synthesized composites on adsorbent performance were studied. Also, the effectiveness of the three morphologies mentioned in the removal of tetracycline and doxycycline (100 mL of 100 mg/L solution in the presence of 7 mg of adsorbents under normal pH conditions was evaluated and compared. The amount of pollutant adsorbed qe (mg/g) and the removal is presented as the results of the designed experiment ( Fig. 1). Eventually, the kinetic experiments and adsorption isotherms were perused.

FT-IR spectrum of GQD, three different types of MIL-88A (Fe), and their synthesized composites are presented in Fig. (S3). In the pure MIL88A spectrum, there is a slight bulge in the range of 3400–3380 cm−1, which can be the result of residual water and washing solvents in the MOF pores. Peaks in 1710–1650 and 1390 cm−1 correspond to symmetric and asymmetric vibration of the carboxyl group in the fumarate ligand, which acts as a linker in the MIL88A structure. Peaks in the range of 1250–1200, 950–900, and 555–600 cm−1, respectively, indicate the presence of the stretching vibrations of C-O, C-H, and Fe-O [60,61].

In the GQD spectrum, a wide, significant peak has appeared at 3350 cm−1, which is related to its surface -OH stretching. In the range of 2800–900 cm−1, C-H vibrations are observed. The wide peak at 1610 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of CO and the other peak at 1450 cm−1 has references to the stretching vibrations of CC. The peak of C-O-C stretching vibrations is observed in 1105 cm−1 [15,62]. In the case of GQDMIL88A composites, the significant broad peak in the range 3400 cm−1 corresponds to GQD surface OH groups and residual water. Peaks at 1400–1600 related to CC and peaks in the range of 1660–1700 and 1390 cm−1 show asymmetric and symmetrical vibration of the carboxyl group in GQD and maybe residual fumaric acid as well, the peak in 555 cm−1 originates from the presence of Fe-O stretching vibrations. C-O-C vibrations appear in 1100 cm−1.

Raman technique can be beneficial for evaluating vibrational and rotational states, even other low frequencies of molecules ( Fig. 2). In the middle wavenumbers of Raman diagrams, some principal peaks can be detected which are related to the G band (in the range of 1640–70 cm−1) and the D band (in the range 1430–1550 cm−1). The D-band relies on A1g respiration of sp3 carbon vibration attached to oxygen-containing functional groups. Also, recent studies have suggested that D-band intensity refers to sp2 to sp3 carbon conversion [63–65] or structural defects presence [66]. While the G -band indicates crystallinity, refers to the first-order scattering of E2g photons and represents sp2 carbon in the structure. According to the Tuinstra-Koening model, the ID/IG ratio corresponds to the carbon material defection, low integrity, and disorder in edges over the main structure (including O-based groups). By computing ID/ IG values of nanocomposites, it was clear that the difference in proportions was quite negligible for the diamond and spindle-like composites (0.773, 0.7695), but for rod-like GQDMIL88A, this ratio was attained at 0.868. This increase in the ratio of ID/ IG indicates that the vacancies and defects on the surface and edge of the r-GQD/MIL88A composite, as well as the surface oxygen groups, are more than other morphologies, probably in the conditions of synthesis in a water media, the possibility of epoxy ring-opening ( in GQD) or MIL88A structural units are more, which leads to a decrease in the intensity of the D- band compared to the G- band (double bonds). By this comparison, it can be concluded that rod-like morphology has more defects compared to spindle-like and diamond-like GQDMIL88A nanocomposite [64,65]. Besides, this process may be explained by the greater solubility of amorphous GQD in the aqueous medium and its greater participation in the reaction with MOF. Examination of peak D intensity can confirm this justification. For more exploration, the r-GQDMIL88A Raman spectrum was explored via the Gaussian function to deconvolute the five peaks (D* D, D′, G, and D″). In the range of (1200–1700 cm−1). The D * and D´ ´ bands which can be seen, are probably attributed to the crystallinity and amount of carbon bonded with sp3, respectively. The D* , D”, D′ bands point to the edge irregularities created by the sp2-sp3 bonds, also, amorphous sp2-carbon bonds, or some vacancy in rings. What is observed after the fitting process indicates that the D" D position is approximately at the same wavelength for all three composite morphologies, although the ID*/IG values of rod-like composite (0.868 vs. 0.769 and 0.7734) can emphasize its lower crystallinity.

The effect of more electrostatic interactions in the rod-like composite is probably the reason for higher D′ band intensity, in this morphology compared to the other two morphologies. In addition, at higher wavelengths (in the ranges of 2700–3200 cm−1), the two-dimensional region is located [67,68].

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images of pure MIL-88A crystals and their composites, to investigate the solvent effect by keeping effective factors on the synthesis (molar ratios, temperature and synthesis methods, and solvent consumption) are shown in ( Fig. 3). The nucleation factor determines the particle size of MIL-88A crystals. In DMF solvent, due to rapid nucleation, abundant but diminutive crystals are produced since the nucleation stage is longer than the growth stage of crystals, thus diamond-like crystals with a diameter of about 700 nm are formed. In contrast, in an aqueous solution, the nucleation stage is concise and the crystal growth takes a long time, so larger particles, rod-like crystals up to 2 µm in diameter are produced. In the case of an equal combination of two solvents, MIL88A crystals with relatively large size, appropriate abundance, and spindle-like have appeared. The diameter of these particles reached 2100 nm contrary to what was mentioned in some previous literature [26,27], the order of diameter and size of MIL88A crystals obtained in various solvents is as follows: r-MIL88A>s- MIL88A>d- MIL88A. This size difference can be elucidated by the surface tension and scrutiny of the solubility of fumaric acid as well as its higher dipole inhibition in DMF (28.6 g fumaric acid in 100.3 mL H2O, μ = 3.86 D) compared to water (0.6 g fumaric acid in 100.3 mLH2O, μ = 1.856 D). Higher dipole momentum affects the solubility of the binders and accelerates the production of MIL-88A nuclei, so more numbers, but smaller sized-MIL88A crystals are generated. In the mixture, sluggish nucleation and giant crystal size are observed. In a mixture of equal proportions of solvents,)H2O 1:1 DMF), a large number of spindle-shaped and medium-size MIL88A can be detected [69]. As can be seen, in GQDMIL88A composites, the presence of GQD on the MIL88A surface is visible. In synthesized composites, the overall structure of all three kinds of MIL-88A is completely preserved and GQD is distributed on the nanocomposite surface. In these nanocomposites, by increasing the GQD, the size of the crystals appears to be somewhat smaller than their precursors (700–500 nm, 2100–1600 nm, and 2–1.7 µm). This shows the effect of increasing GQD on three kinds of MIL88A structures [57].

TEM images of GQD ( Fig. 4a-b) indicate circular, monodisperse nanoparticles with a graphical structure [55,56]. The images of d-GQDMIL88A and s-GQDMIL88A (Fig. 4c-g), reveal the presence of GQD in MIL88A. The architectural MIL88A structure is almost preserved after the GQDs are embedded in the nanocomposite. Also, at higher GQD contents, the composite surface is slightly rough due to the agglomeration of GQDs on the MIL88A surface, but the architectural stability remains [27,40].

The MAP and EDX images of all three kinds of pure MIL88A and their composites are illustrated in Fig. S1 (Supplementary File). The proper dispersion of the elements present (C, Fe, O, Cl) in the synthesized samples is a confirmation of their high purity and efficient synthesis [70].

To inquire about the crystal shape and structure of the synthesized samples, their XRD was examined along with other identification methods ( Fig. 5). The XRD pattern confirms the crystallinity and purity of these products. The previous article has described the morphological differences of the MIL88A due to the change in the size of the crystal plates [27]. Although the 100 and 101 surfaces have received the most attention in this regard as the main reason for this morphological difference in MIL88A, the exact position of these surfaces is presented with some inconsistencies. Some articles attribute the peaks of the range (2θ = 8–10º) [26,53,69] and others the peaks of the region (2θ =10–12º) [27,61] to these surfaces. Interestingly, in some articles and this work in the range of 8–12º (both ranges mentioned), the corresponding peaks are observed [70]. In another article, changes in three-dimensional MIL-88 x-ray diffraction examines from another perspective. The changes observed at the XRD peak location have been due to the high swelling of the hybrid frameworks and the change in MOF cell volume up to almost double. Since in the swelling process, an unstable transfer process occurs, this phenomenon is not associated with breaking any bonds, the topology of the frameworks is fully preserved and only a change in the intensity and displacement of the area of the peak is observed [71]. Further growth in different crystalline surfaces causes the structural diversity of MIL88A to the three types mentioned. Given what has been mentioned [71], the effect of solvent on MIL88A synthesis and possible swelling can be a reason for this diversity in growth and development. As can be seen in the SEM images, the ratio of the solvents used in the synthesis causes a significant change in the appearance of MIL88A. The XRD patterns in the range of 2θ = 10º are attributed to the 100, (2θ =11–12º) to 101, and 2θ = 13º to 002 crystallographic facets. As the DMF increases, the funds expand from (100) to (101), so rod, spindle, and diamond shapes of MIL88A are created [26,27]. According to (Fig. 5a), in the optimal composite with the lowest GQD content, there is still no noticeable change in the crystal structure of MIL88A. In the case of the synthesized composites, despite the amorphous structure of GQDs, the main peaks of the pure r-MIL-88A (Fe) are preserved even in composites with higher GQD quantities. The created width and change in position and intensity of some peaks, especially in composites containing higher GQD (5–11 wt%) content, can confirm the presence of this material in the structure of MIL-88 (Fe). This event may indicate, that GQD doping can alter the structure of MOFs to some extent [9,58].

The surface area, volume, and size of the pores were evaluated for all three synthesized GQDMIL88A composites and the results were presented below ( Table 1, Fig. 6). Depending on the type of the synthesized composite, according to the IUPAC classification, the adsorption curves for r-GQDMIL88 (Fe), s-GQDMIL88 (Fe), and d-GQDMIL88 (Fe) are obtained, indicating that these three types of GQDMIL88A followed the type IV isotherm. The adsorption behavior of mesopores is such that first multi-layer adsorption and then pore condensation occurs. Adsorbent-adsorbate interactions, as well as the interactions of adsorbent molecules in the compression state, determine this liquid-vapor phase transition process. When the pore width, in this case, exceeds a specified critical width, the pore condensation can be accompanied by hysteresis. As can be seen, type three hysterics are followed here. In H3-type hysteresis, despite the strength in the adsorption process, various phenomena such as blockage of pores, cavitation, or evaporation of pores in network structures, cause delays in the desorption process [72]. Interestingly, the specific surface area of r-GQDMIL88 (Fe), s-GQDMIL88 (Fe), and d-GQDMIL88 (Fe) were 14.82 m2/g, 10.78 m2/g, and 47.239 m2/g, respectively (Table 1). Fig. 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of operational parameters on dye adsorption

ce:section.ce:para: The results of the various GQD content of nanocomposite on the dye removal performance in Fig. 8 are presented. One of the effective parameters of adsorption can be to find the optimal mixing ratio of components in composite preparation. As observed, GQDMIL88A (1%) showed the highest amount of pollutant adsorption. While increasing the amount of graphene quantum dot in the composite structure (5–11%) is associated with a decrease in the amount of contaminant adsorption. It can be interpreted that by increasing the GQD content, the contaminant access to the surface, cavities, and functional groups of the MOF decreases, thus reducing the effect of the MOF on the adsorption process, and it may the adsorption process is more affected by GQDs. While at lower content (GQDMIL88A 1%), MIL88A and GQD are in constructive balances, which improves the overall performance of the composite in the adsorption process [36,52].

The effect of initial contaminant concentration on the removal process was explored using various types of MIL88A and their composites with the desired GQD content at the optimum adsorbent dose, pH= 4, and at ambient temperature (Fig. S5). The adsorption rate of AB92 on the synthesized materials increases over time and eventually reaches equilibrium. As the concentration of contaminants increases, the percentage of removal in all three types of MIL88A and their composites decreases. At lower concentrations of pollutants, the ratio of AB92 molecules to active adsorbent sites is more appropriate, adsorption will also be more effective until the adsorption process is completely balanced. At higher concentrations of pollutants, under constant adsorbent dose, the limited and insufficient active sites saturate rapidly, resulting in a decrease in removal rate [73,74].

Pollutant solution pH is a noteworthy issue that can be decisive in the absorption process. It was investigated by changing the pH from 4 to 10 in 100 mL of AB92 solution (20 mg/L). As can be seen in Fig. 9 with increasing pH from 4 to 10 efficiency decreases dramatically from 92% to 20%. Since AB 92 dissociation constant (pKa) was 3.2 [75], and it is an anionic dye-containing negative sulfonic group (SO3 -), at pH= 4.0, the main driving force can be the strong electrostatic attraction between the anionic form of the dye and the positively charged composites surface. At higher pH, increasing the concentration of -OH ions reduce the positive charge and even creates a negative charge on the adsorbent surface, so a decrease in gravity and even repulsion can be a reasonable justification for this decrease in adsorption [76,77]. Determining the amount of charge of particles is possible by analyzing Zeta Potential. The value of particle charge can cause their stability, preventing their sedimentation and agglomeration, and ion adsorption process. In this regard, a zeta potential test was performed to determine the surface charges of GQDMIL88A at different pHs (2.4, 4.5, 9.1, 10, and 11). The surface charges reported are in good agreement with the information obtained in the adsorption process of AB 92, as well as previous reports [78]. As Fig. 9 indicates the positive surface charge at pH values less than 9, with the most positive surface state in the range of pH = 4, which is the optimal pH reported for anionic dye adsorption. At higher pH (more than pH =9), the negative surface charge of the composite has caused repulsion and reduced adsorption to 26.1%.

Different types of GQDMIL-88A (Fe (rod-like, spindle-like, and diamond-like exhibited different adsorption rates. With an increasing dose of GQDMIL88A (Fe(, from 2 to 9 mg, the AB92 removal rate also increases. It is due to the enhancement in specific surface areas and the availability of active adsorption sites. From 2–7 mg the adsorption process increases significantly, but no considerable increase is observed in the amount of 9 mg. This can be due to the agglomeration of the adsorbent. With the accumulation of adsorbents, access to their effective sites may become more difficult, and as a result, a significant improvement in the amount of adsorption may not occur. Finally, the choice of 7 mg as the optimal dose was best for comparing different composite types [79,80]. Fig. 10.

Herein, three isotherm models of AB92 adsorption by synthesized adsorbents, GQD, MIL88A (spindle, rod, and diamond-like), and GQDMIL88A (spindle, rod, and diamond-like) at room temperature were studied. Descriptions of the mentioned isotherms and diagrams for their evaluation are shown in Fig. S3-S6 (Supplementary file). Considering higher R2 values for MIL88A (r: 0.9858, s: 0.9661, d: 0.9665), also GQDMIL88A (r: 0.9855, s: 0.9448, d: 0.9743), and the information obtained, dye adsorption for MOFs and their nanocomposites follows the Langmuir isotherm (Table S3 and Figs. (S3-S6)), After the studies, we found that based on the isotherm model obtained for all three composite morphologies (the Langmuir), single-layer adsorption on a homogeneous site can occur at the adsorbent surface. Insomuch the pollutant molecules are adsorbed by well-defined and active sites on the adsorbent surface, any active site can adsorb only a layer of the pollutant molecules.

To obtain information about the adsorption mechanism, control of this process at the surface, diffusion performance, and possible chemical reactions of the adsorption kinetics are evaluated [81,82]. The physical and chemical features of adsorbents are influential factors in kinetic models and ultimately play an evaluating role in the rate of reactions [83]. Herein, various kinetics of AB92 adsorption on the synthesized adsorbents were investigated.

According to the higher R2 value for MIL88A (r: 0.9967, s: 0.994, d: 0.9956) and GQDMIL88A (r: 0.9942, s: 0.9998, d: 0.9998 can be argued that all the various shapes of MIL88A and their composites are well compatible with a pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Table S4 and Fig. S7-S10). Therefore, the mechanism of adsorption depends on two factors. These kinetic models were not able to provide the desired information about particle diffusion, the intraparticle diffusion model was examined too. This model refers to the two-step adsorption process [84,85]. Since adsorption kinetics is related to the chemical and physical properties of the synthesized adsorbents, and AB 92 adsorption followed a pseudo-second-order kinetic model, it can be concluded that both the concentration of the contaminant and the adsorbent dose affect the reaction rate and the interactions between the all three morphologies of GQDMIL88A composite as the adsorbent and the contaminant molecules. More details about the adsorption kinetics are discussed in the supplementary file.

Considering the adsorbent, adsorbate structure, and adsorption media, numerous mechanisms can be effective in the adsorption process which sometimes, one or multiple interactions occur. Among these, it can mention the electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, the influence of framework metal, acid-base accumulation, etc. [11,12].

To study the mechanism of adsorption, functional groups affecting the adsorption process, including hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amine, must be considered. These groups provide the basis for physical interactions through hydrogen bonds in the adsorption process. Since MIL-88A (Fe) contains the structure of μ - OH and GQD in the composite, both are susceptible to hydrogen bonding, and given that previous studies have confirmed that μ - OH MOF [86] and GQDs functional groups play a role in the adsorption and diffusion processes. All contaminants studied in this work (AB92, TC, and Dox) contain hydrogen bonding-prone functional groups. To inquire about functional groups' effect on the adsorption of AB 92, some of these groups were individually protected by chemical modification. To protect these functional groups, ethylene glycol, for the carbonyl group and benzyl chloride to protect the hydroxyl and amine functional group were used with a molar ratio of 1.5 times the mentioned functional groups [87,88]. The results of this study in Fig. 11 indicate that no so significant decrease (4%, 14.3%, and 35.2%) occurred in this process and the zeta potential test and pH effect data reflect this point that π-π and electrostatic interactions may be the determining mechanisms of this pollutant adsorption [50,89].

Electrostatic interaction has been repeatedly confirmed as one of the most common mechanisms in the adsorption process of contaminants from water. MOFs can interact with contaminants with opposite charges easily, while the MOF surface charge appertains to the pH of the solution. The optimum pH of the AB92 adsorption process was obtained at 4.0. The dissociation constant of this dye (pKa) is 3.2 [75]. Thus it can be concluded that at the desired pH, dye exists in ionic mode. Hence, electrostatic interactions with MIL88A and GQD of their composites can be one of the proposed adsorption mechanisms. Prominent features of TCs are diffused ring systems with numerous functional groups which can be ionized and lead to multiple equilibrium constants (pKa). These compounds usually have three pKa. The first pKa (pKa1) belongs to the three carbonyl amide groups (C1 -C3) and the second (pKa2) to the phenolic diction group, and the third (pKa3) is attributed to nitrogen in the dimethyl amino group. Therefore, TCs are prone to three types of presence di anionic (TC−2) and anionic (TC−), cationic (TCH+), neutral (TC0), in aqueous solution [90–92].

Typically, π-π interactions/ stacking occurs in electron-deficient or electron-rich systems. Considering the chemical structures of AB92, tetracycline, and doxycycline and the synthesized nanomaterials, the presence of several aromatic rings and unsaturated bonds in the mentioned structures certainly confirm the possibility of π-π receptor and donor occurrence in the adsorption process [11,12,84].

Clusters of metal ions in the structure of MOFs can also be responsible for adsorption interactions. A study by Francisco José Romero-Salguero and Dolores Esquivel, comparing several MOFs, introduced the effect of Fe-π interaction as the main and effective factor of ethylene uptake by MIL88A [93]. Fe-π interaction can be our next proposed mechanism for the pollutant adsorption process. In general, the possible mechanisms of AB92, TC, or Dox adsorption are presented in Fig. 12.

Despite quite similar functional groups of the three forms of MIL88A and their composites and the contaminant medium with the same conditions, relying on the proposed mechanisms alone is not sufficient to justify the adsorption events of this study. To justify the adsorption differences of the various shapes, we encountered interesting turning points. On the one hand, the specific surface area presented in Table 1 for synthesized composites was 47.2 > 14.9 > 10.8 m2/g for d-GQDMIL88A, r-GQDMIL88A, s-GQDMIL88A respectively. The highest adsorption belonged to the s-GQDMIL88A composite with the lowest specific surface area of 10.8 m2/g, although similar cases have been reported before [94]. On the other hand, for s-GQDMIL88A composite the maximum pore size of about 29 nm has been presented compared to other cases, so the greater ability of the contaminant to penetrate the composite pores could have been a promising justification for interpreting the high adsorption capacity of this adsorbent [95,96]. But d-GQDMIL88A with a medium pore size (17 nm) possesses a lower absorption capacity comparing r-GQDMIL88A for AB92 and Dox adsorption, so emphasis on this theory alone does not make sense. Recently, evaluating the performance of compounds with the same structure but different shapes, which is caused by different growth of their crystal surfaces, has become a controversial challenge, and this is the turning point of the present study. Based on all the available evidence, the appearance of nanomaterials resulting from different growth of their crystalline surface can be a justification for the phenomenon of difference in adsorption for various MIL88 shapes [27]. Two commensurate crystalline surfaces can increase the MIL potential for more efficient adsorption of contaminants. The s-GQDMIL88 composite, in which 100 and 101 crystal surfaces are proportionally grown and have the largest pore size, offers better adsorption efficiency than the other two morphologies by utilizing two salient factors. However, the rod-like composite, with the largest 100 crystalline surface area, and much smaller pore size, and d-GQDMIL88A, with the expanded 101 crystalline surface and the average pore size, showed no impressive adsorption differences in TC and Dox removal.

The structural stability of adsorbents in aqueous media before and after the adsorption process is one of the most critical aspects that is considered to treat water pollutants [97]. Since the reuse ability of spent adsorbents is a controversial challenge in assessing their importance in industry and commerce, this feature is a turning point in most research in evaluating the applicability of the adsorbents. In this work, eluent for AB92 molecules loaded in the adsorbents was considered 0.1 M HCl: EtOH (1:9, v /v). According to Fig. 13, the adsorption capacity of the composites was not substantially reduced. This emphasizes the high stability of the synthesized nanocomposite and this issue refers to the reversibility of the pollutant adsorption process and structural evolution in aqueous solutions that confirms during the repeated recovery process, the adsorbents were well regenerated after rinsing with ethanol and remained stable [98]. This could confirm the hypothesis that the van der Waals or other forces such as the π-π or π-Fe interactions or electrostatic attraction, and hydrogen bonding are the forces influencing the adsorption process [11,99,100].

In the present study, different morphologies of the novel, efficient and reusable GQD/MIL88A (Fe) heterostructure have been successfully synthesized and utilized as the adsorbents for Acid Blue 92, tetracycline, and doxycycline removal. Differences in physicochemical properties and even significant changes in adsorption performance, for different morphologies are noteworthy results of this research. But, in all cases, the Langmuir isotherm and Pseudo-second order kinetics are the best descriptions for the adsorption process. The adsorption of the monolayer is the predominant model. Also, by the available evidence, among the possible adsorption mechanisms, electrostatic interactions, the influence of framework metal, π-π interactions stacking, and hydrogen bonding were suggested. According to the achievements of this research, it turned out that, the MOF adsorption performance is not only influenced by the active sites in their chemical structure but also features such as shape, differences in the growth of their different crystalline surfaces, (rod, spindle, diamond-like), sizes, specific surface area, even pore diameter, and volume can play the undeniable role in this process. Since in the spindle-like structure, both crystal surface (100,101) has grown proportional and the size of the pores has been larger to penetrate the contaminant into the MOF pores, has created the most talented state for the maximum amount of adsorption. To our knowledge, this work is the first report in which the adsorption behavior of a specific substance with the well-controlled shape MOF and its composites has been inquired about in detail. Also, this research provided a new notion to design the adsorption process and predict the efficiency of adsorbents using different morphologies.
